title: THE ANIMAL OF OUR BODIES
text: THE ANIMAL OF OUR BODIES by Kristi DeMeester W e put the candles in the room on a Tuesday afternoon. We close the door. We lock it. You look at me like you did the night of our wedding, all pride and triumph, and I bare my teeth at you in a way that is supposed to be a smile. I am the animal to your soft curves. The blood to your swollen skin. In the pseudo-safety of our bed, you curl into yourself, eyes closed, mouth drawn in a tight line, as if these could be totems to ward off a haunting. But this is the house of my girlhood. The laughter in the night, the screaming, all of it is mine. The ghost is me. My fury and sadness and confusion bound by the walls and made manifest. “The candles will help. Like an offering,” you whisper, and I curl into you, take your warmth until you are shivering. “You read it in a book,” I say, not a question. You nod, and I trace my teeth along your almond-scented throat. I want to apologize. To tell you I never wanted this. But it would be a lie. This house is stained by all the things I swallowed down until they were more poison than secret. I never imagined myself locked behind its doors again, but my father died, and we were isolating like every other millennial couple during this fucking pandemic. It was easier to move our things out of our shitty apartment and into a place with a paid mortgage and furniture that didn’t come unassembled in a box. We’d both managed to survive downsizing, but our paychecks hadn’t been stretching far enough for years. You said the house was a sort of miracle. Prayers and incantations are the same, aren’t they? So a miracle can’t be that far from a curse. The same words in different order. You sleep in fits and starts, and I stare at the door, which I know I closed but is now standing wide open. I tell myself there is nothing standing in the hallway with fingers curled around the doorway so it might peek in at us. “I hope you can see us,” I whisper to the thing I’m stupidly pretending isn’t there, hoping I can offer it a light to move into. That in seeing us together, it can finally move on. Its anger leaking back into the floors, the walls, the doors as it finally escapes into a peace it had never known. It flees, a darker smear against the night shadows. I turn back to you, and you press against me, and I would absorb you if I could. The ghost in the hall understands. She knows what it is to ache from a loss that hasn’t happened yet. To sharpen her teeth even as she longs for a softness that never existed in this house. In the morning, your eyes are bloodshot as you down your coffee and boot up for another day of pointless Zoom meetings, and I am guilty of being the wolf at your door. I lured you here with false promises of domestic bliss and big eyes and ears and teeth. But there is no rest in this house, and I know I’m to blame for it all. I rub your shoulders as a kind of weak apology, and you shrug me off. Your meeting starts in a few minutes, and you need to go through your email. I tell myself you are tired and frightened, but I am alone even with you here. I should be working too, contributing to our family unit, but I drift through the rooms, scratch at the walls until my fingers are numb. There is no ghost in the plaster, but I see it in the periphery, can hear it sobbing even with the candles we put in the room, even with it seeing us together. Your headphones are in, but your shoulders are stiff, and I know you hear it too. Your candles didn’t work. Houses aren’t supposed to be haunted in the daylight, but this one is. This is the point where we abandon everything before the sighs manifest into raised, burning scratches along our backs, but there is death outside, and we have nowhere else to go. I hear the girl version of myself crying while I am making lunch. Somewhere, the phantasm of my father is shouting at her, his hand gripping her collar as he pours out his hatred and knowledge of sin. If I close my eyes, I can feel how he bleached out my heart little by little. “Tuna?” I ask, and your eyes are large. Wet. You heard it, too, but you don’t know the sound of my girl voice yet. I clench the butter knife in my hand as if it could do anything other than scrape. “It didn’t work. The candles,” you say, and I set down the knife because I can’t trust my own hands. Not in this house. “Maybe you read it wrong,” I say. You shake your head, and your hair is all shimmering gold tipped in rose. “We should use salt. At the doors.” I turn and pretend to be putting away the bread. It won’t work. My ghost drowned in salt, her face tear-slick even as she pressed fingers to the veins at her wrist, wondering if she could ever be strong enough to open herself up and let the pain stream out of her. Upstairs a door slams, and you jump and then laugh. “This is the point we’re supposed to call in a priest, right?” “Maybe they can wear one of those masks. Those plague doctor ones. Seems only appropriate.” You shiver, and I wrap you up, your breath hot against my collarbone. “I’ll sell it once this is all over. Back to normal.” It’s a lie, but you sigh. “I don’t think normal is a thing anymore.” You drift back to your laptop, to your headphones. You pick at the sandwich I make you, and I eventually throw it away. Another thing wasted by this house. The house is quiet for the rest of the day, but there is a subtle heaviness to the air. As if the settling dust has taken on weight, burying itself in our lungs until we are left coughing blood and struggling to breathe. Finally you close your laptop, and I bring you a glass of the overly sweet wine you love but I hate, and the floor is gritty with the salt you poured at the thresholds. The air tastes of the ocean, and I bring my tongue to my lips again and again, thinking of how later, in the dark, I’ll kneel between your legs in worship. I stroke your hair and watch as you swallow. I can feel the ghost upstairs expand like a gust of wind, a groan caught in its non-existent throat. You don’t hear it this time, but your arms break out in goose bumps. “They updated the county numbers today. Ninety more positive. Ten dead,” you say. “You have to stop reading the news. It’s morbid.” You shrug. Sip your wine. “So is the entire fucking world right now.” Somewhere in the house, a door opens, and there is the sound of something small moving carefully. You freeze, your glass lifted halfway to your lips, your eyes darting to the scattered line of salt. “I didn’t put any upstairs yet.” “We can sleep down here tonight if you want. The couch pulls out.” “Okay. Yeah.” You nod. From upstairs comes a muffled scream, the sound of someone with a hand clamped over her mouth, and then a thud. I’ve read that a poltergeist is a kind of imprint of what’s come before, but I carry too many identical memories to identify which transgression this sound belongs to. “There are spare blankets in the downstairs closet,” I say and stand. You stay where you are, your shoulders rigid as you listen for another reminder that I’ve brought you into a horror story. Somehow, you are even more beautiful when you are frightened, and I want to pull you onto the floor and tug off your panties, but you wouldn’t allow it. Not now. Not with a ghost over our heads, and the secret of what it is burning inside me. There was never any question of your being out. You grew up surrounded by love, parents who looked at you and saw their own hearts walking through the world. There were girlfriends before me. Other people who loved you. It was inevitable. I’m harder to love than you, and I think you saw me as something to be conquered. You could not have known how I once dreamed of living the truth you’d always known, the lost girl in me gnashing her teeth in hunger. The blankets in the closet smell of mothballs, and you wrinkle your nose as I shake them out and smooth the edges over the couch’s small mattress. We settle in, the television casting blue shadows over the room. You asked me to leave it on, the muted susurrations a semblance of safety. Eventually, you slip into a restless sleep, so I turn the television off and wait. I don’t have to wait long. The energy in the room shifts, the air gone thick in the way I recognize, and I close my eyes. Tell myself that whatever I see will not be a horror. “It’s only me,” I whisper and open my eyes into the dark. It’s standing in front of me, its mouth open and hanging loose, the jaw unhinged from the socket. It appears as a girl, as me at twelve, but it isn’t. It smells of ozone. “He broke our jaw. I remember,” I say. It tilts its head and reaches for me, tangles its fingers in my hair, which I’d finally cut short. Even after I’d left this house, my father, behind, I’d think of cutting it and feel hellfire licking at my feet for daring to consider such an abomination. A woman’s hair was her modesty. Her glory. It drops its hand, and its mouth works uselessly, a thin whine fading into a hiss. “You can leave now. He’s dead,” I say, but it looks beyond me to you. You turn over and sigh, and the sound breaks me open in the way it always does. I blink, and it is beside you, its face lowered to your lips, its mouth impossibly wide as it breathes in. Tasting you. Pulling you into itself and away from me. I scream your name, scramble toward you, and you come awake with a gasp, your hands clutching my arm as my ghost disappears back into the dark. “It’s okay. Just a bad dream,” I say as you burrow into my chest, saying again and again that you want out of this fucking house. I don’t tell you what I heard. What the ghost said just as you were coming awake. Mine . # You’ve stopped eating. You pretend to be full after a few bites and spend the rest of our paltry meals asking me question after question as if by filling your mouth with words, I won’t notice how you aren’t filling it with food. The house has been quiet, and we’ve gone back to the bedroom. You sleep clinging to the edge, every part of your body arching away from me, and I wrap the blanket around me, but it’s a paltry substitution for the heat of your body. You log in to your Zoom meetings, but you are quiet, letting the conversations, the work, exist without you. One morning, I catch you dumping your once beloved coffee down the sink, and I hold my hand to your head. You swat it away and frown. “I’m not sick.” “Something’s off. We should get you tested.” “For what exactly? We haven’t left this fucking house in weeks.” “The grocery deliveries. Something could have come in.” You snort and turn away. “I’m not getting my brain swabbed over nothing.” “People are dying. It’s not nothing.” “I said I’m fine. Jesus Christ.” I recoil from the force in your voice, and for a moment, it isn’t you but my father, the force of his hand bearing down on me. My heart accelerates, the room going fuzzy at the corners, and I hold onto the counter instead of you and tell myself I will not have a panic attack. He’s dead. He isn’t here. When I finally look up, you are gone, but the ghost is standing at the top of the stairs, its broken mouth pulled into an obscene smile. Our bedroom door closes, and I am frozen as I watch it turn, its steps sounding down the hallway. I take the stairs one by one and listen for your scream, but the house is quiet. If the ghost of me is in the room with you, it hasn’t revealed itself. No shattered mirrors or flickering lights or sighs. Only you, undressing while it watches and imagining what it would feel like to rake its teeth along your throat, your scent on its blackened tongue. A reminder of the things I, we , so desperately wanted but was the forbidden fruit. But when I throw open the door, there is no ghost leaning over you, and you glance up at me. “If you’re going to keep lecturing me—” you start, and I swallow your words with my mouth on yours. There is no relief in it because the room is heavy with my ghost. From the walls, from the floorboards, it whispers, and I know what it wants. What it’s always wanted. Because I finally have it. Later, after I have claimed every inch of you, you sleep, and I slip out of bed and press myself against the floor. “You can’t have her,” I say, and from inside the house, the ghost of me laughs. We are an ouroboros of desire and degradation. How can I keep it from what we already have? Haven’t I already destroyed you by letting you fall in love with me? Even still, I repeat it over and over, holding to the phrase as if it could ward off evil. For the rest of the night, the ghost keeps away, but I can feel it moving through the house. I don’t sleep. In the morning, you sweep up the salt and collect the candles. “What are you doing?” I ask, and you dump the salt into the trash. “It wasn’t working anyway. Besides, it’s not like it’s hurting us.” You move slowly, as if you are trapped in a dream. “I’m going back to bed,” you say, and you sleep through the afternoon, your phone pinging nonstop with emails and messages. It isn’t like you to ignore work. For hours, I sit beside you watching your chest rise and fall. In the evening, I try to wake you. “Too tired,” you mumble, and I bring you toast and tea clouded with milk. You drink the tea and crumble the toast into bits, and your eyes are hollow as you stare at me. “The house is so lovely. Why did you leave?” “I thought you wanted me to sell it,” I say, and you snuggle down into the blanket. “It’s like living in a book. A Gothic Romance.” You smile, ignoring that such books tend to end in blood. If we are haunted, you’ve evolved into a willing conduit. Your body an altar. An offering not for the dead but for the girl left behind. “You wouldn’t love her if you knew,” I whisper. You tell me you didn’t hear what I said, and I tell you it’s nothing. Not important. I will not let what’s infected this house seduce you. You stay in bed all of the next day, drifting in and out, your body looking insubstantial. You have lost weight, and your skin looks as if you could slip out of it. I want to cry for you, but the tears belong only to my ghost. In this house, they have always been hers. Once night falls, I press my lips to your forehead and close the door behind me. I find your candles. The salt. A knife meant for separating meat from bone. I carry all these things into my old bedroom. The one tucked at the bottom of the stairs, the door all but hidden behind the dresser my father dragged in front of it. The room is as I left it. Spare with its twin bed and single pine dresser. No posters. No pictures. The only ornament the heavy crucifix hanging on the far wall. I could almost laugh with the irony of it. I’m surprised the crucifix hasn’t inverted itself. A haunting growing out of the room my father tried to make holy with his violence. He’d wanted the crucifix as a reminder Jesus was always watching, but all I ever wanted to do was stab myself with it. I pour the salt in a circle and set the candles just outside the perimeter. Squeezing my eyes shut, I pass the knife over my palm, the pain flaring and then dying into a dull ache. The candlelight flickers, casting strange shadows against the wall. I let my blood drop to the floor before stepping inside the circle. I do not turn when I hear the door creak open, do not look when I hear its tongue lapping at my— our —spilled blood, but eventually, it creeps before me on all fours, its head bent low, its tongue still trailing along the floor. “You don’t have to do this. You’ve seen what happens. You leave. You fall in love. You get married,” I say, and I want to scream until my throat is raw. Looking into its face, this younger version of myself, is unnatural. Obscene. I clutch the knife, but there is no comfort in it. No safety. My ghost shifts backward, its eyes gleaming in the candlelight, and shakes its head. Its voice is guttural, each syllable scraping past its teeth. “Give her to me.” “Fuck you.” It laughs. Reaches out a hand and scatters the salt and candles. “Stupid,” it says. The house has always belonged to it. To the ghost of the girl I was before. To her anger. There is no protection here. Already, it has begun its absorption of you, and I could take you away from here. Go to a hotel if they would take us. But you are weak from not eating. Susceptible. I can’t deliver you from one death into another, and how do you banish a ghost that never died? How do you exorcise evil created from a fervency of belief? “Please,” I say, and there is no magic in that word. How many times did it pass my lips even as my father sweated red-faced over me, my body breaking under his version of justice? From upstairs, I hear you call my name, and my ghost turns toward the sound and vanishes. I do scream then as I scramble to my feet, stumble over the salt, the candles, and run toward the stairs. I call your name, but all is quiet and still. The air is oppressive and pushes against me as I reach the top and call your name. You don’t answer, and the silence is an awful sort of violence. I would take you into me, wrap you in the meat of my body and keep you safe. Anything to keep you with me. Anything. Ahead of me our bedroom door opens. The darkness inside shifting like something alive, and I force myself on even though I am shaking because you are inside with it. With the anger and longing I tried to leave behind. But intentions always leave a residue, don’t they? We cannot erase who we were. It is a dormant beast. Again I call your name as I enter our room. You are sitting in the middle of our bed, the blankets nested around you, and my chest aches with how beautiful you are. “What are you doing?” you ask, and your eyes are huge. Luminous in the dark. Like my ghost. “Get out of her.” I clench my hands into fists, but my hands have always been weak. I have never been able to fight back. Your throat and chest swell impossibly, the skin stretching to make room for what should not be, and I let out a sob. Too late. Always too late. My life has been a sum of all the ways a person can be powerless. But you are mine. Our vows etched on my skin, thrumming in my blood, and I am the wailing in the night. The teeth in the dark. You are meant only for me. I kneel over you, and your eyes widen. “What are you doing?” you ask again. There is panic in your voice, but it’s your voice, and I kiss you once. “I have to. You don’t understand.” “You don’t. It’s not—” The knife I still carry is sharp. You part under me easily as I cut myself out of you. You scream, your hands beating against me, but I won’t let it have you. Around me, the house shudders, and the room expands and contracts like the chamber of some terrible heart. My hands are slick with you, but the air is clear. My ghost purged. I touch your face, your hair, your mouth, wait for you to draw me to you, but your hands are limp at your sides. Your body still. “No,” I say. If my ghost were still here, it would laugh. I place my mouth over yours, but your lungs won’t take my air, and I howl as I claw at your chest, as I scream your name into the emptied house. I reach into the thin cage of your chest and touch the heart you gave me. Your body is my body. Your blood is my blood. And there are teeth, and teeth, and teeth. I take you into me, and we are bound in this way. A communion of blood and flesh. I can only hope, even as I swallow your heart, that you will haunt me. Please. Haunt me. Robert E. Howard's footprint on horror fiction in particular and dark speculative fiction in general is large and imposing, and one of the things I've always loved about his work is the slippery line he treads between horror and fantasy, creating some of the most enduring horror-fantasy stories ever written. Especially his horror/weird westerns. With “O Adelín,” Michael Wehunt brings his own darkly magical horror/weird western, which isn't surprising, given his places in the pages of Year's Best Dark Fantasy and Horror and Year's Best Horror Fiction. I truly hope this won't be the only time he graces the pages of Liminal Spaces.
